*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Grace_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Mount%20Grace%20Priory/@54.3833,-1.2833,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZOlMoqPf0FvVcAWK3Mkcb0pgYMrNE5gNJ_ADPs6q1oCipOdhlUV7pszzgtzv9rWHVjVK4HShX5mYeaZJboVht-9ebIC9lOHp3sU1TsKGKUjCcRJsCEnBBXzRmxylq22pCsRTEsGxOJXSWfh=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Mount Grace Priory is a monastery in the parish of East Harlsey, North Yorkshire, England. Set in woodlands within the North York Moors National Park, it is represented today by the best preserved and most accessible ruins among the nine houses of the Carthusian Order, which existed in England in the Middle Ages and were known as charterhouses.

_Listing 2 of 50_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Rippon Cathedral
*Country:* england
*Region:* yorkshire
*County:* Yorkshire
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 7th century / 12th-16th century
*Coords:* 54.13472222, -1.52
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ripon_Cathedral
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Rippon%20Cathedral/@54.1381,-1.5217,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Ripon_Cathedral_Exterior%2C_Nth_Yorkshire%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/330px-Ripon_Cathedral_Exterior%2C_Nth_Yorkshire%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://riponcathedral.org.uk/

*Description:*
The Cathedral Church of St Peter and St Wilfrid, commonly known as Ripon Cathedral, and until 1836 known as Ripon Minster, is a cathedral in Ripon, North Yorkshire, England. Founded as a monastery by monks of the Irish tradition in the 660s, it was refounded as a Benedictine monastery by St Wilfrid in 672. The church became collegiate in the tenth century, and acted as a mother church within the large Diocese of York for the remainder of the Middle Ages. The present church is the fourth, and was built between the 13th and 16th centuries. In 1836 the church became the cathedral for the Diocese of Ripon. In 2014 the Diocese was incorporated into the new Diocese of Leeds, and the church became one of three co-equal cathedrals of the Bishop of Leeds.

_Listing 3 of 50_

listing.name: Rippon Cathedral
listing.desc: The Cathedral Church of St Peter and St Wilfrid, commonly known as Ripon Cathedral, and until 1836 known as Ripon Minster, is a cathedral in Ripon, North Yorkshire, England. Founded as a monastery by monks of the Irish tradition in the 660s, it was refounded as a Benedictine monastery by St Wilfrid in 672. The church became collegiate in the tenth century, and acted as a mother church within the large Diocese of York for the remainder of the Middle Ages. The present church is the fourth, and was built between the 13th and 16th centuries. In 1836 the church became the cathedral for the Diocese of Ripon. In 2014 the Diocese was incorporated into the new Diocese of Leeds, and the church became one of three co-equal cathedrals of the Bishop of Leeds.

imageAttribution.description: Ripon Cathedral's western facade as viewed from Minster Rd in North Yorkshire, England.

*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNI8yAIRwtMIsjGf1Hwb_LKvMv7mdpEspVLA4o4jyruc1dw4PkgyV4CZ-ec-ztCAOKmBueShmhkDotqtNuUqrtZyVUv7cdaLqruwwYrIGFyGeyN-sydeit1_tjfH21B2zU_Z4-wH759n9dBuJY=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/KqCDQkgK3R1xfe3S7


*Description:*
Brodsworth Hall, near Brodsworth, 5 miles (8 km) north-west of Doncaster in South Yorkshire, is one of the most complete surviving examples of a Victorian country house in England. It is virtually unchanged since the 1860s. It was designed in the Italianate style by the obscure London architect, Philip Wilkinson, then 26 years old. He was commissioned by Charles Sabine Augustus Thellusson, who inherited the estate in 1859, but the original estate was constructed in 1791 for merchant and slave owner Peter Thellusson. It is a Grade I listed building.

_Listing 4 of 50_

listing.name: Brodsworth Hall
listing.desc: Brodsworth Hall, near Brodsworth, 5 miles (8 km) north-west of Doncaster in South Yorkshire, is one of the most complete surviving examples of a Victorian country house in England. *Country:* england
*Region:* yorkshire
*County:* Yorkshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 18th century
*Coords:* 53.6533, -1.3883
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nostell_Priory
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Nostell%20Priory/@53.6533,-1.3883,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Nostell_Priory_Front_Facade.JPG/330px-Nostell_Priory_Front_Facade.JPG
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Nostell Priory is a Palladian house in Nostell, West Yorkshire, in England, near Crofton and on the road to Doncaster from Wakefield. It dates from 1733 and was built for the Winn family on the site of a medieval priory. The Priory and its contents were given to the National Trust in 1953 by the trustees of the estate and Rowland Winn, 3rd Baron St Oswald.

_Listing 5 of 50_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Bryn Celli Ddu
*Country:* wales
*Region:* snowdonia north
*County:* Anglesey
*Type:* monument
*Period:* Neolithic
*Coords:* 53.2077, -4.2361
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bryn_Celli_Ddu
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Bryn%20Celli%20Ddu/@53.2072,-4.2361,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZPiVWPA6MKBzVoHlcp23V1CmirXCEgUKOlcyUjAHRCqDXf2ZK4Qc_p5h6KhNqW1BPr3p-h3KL3fF0Y60CcZhF3QKWN8I_t6gIBlVzdEjjj0QQWMaVr4jksXyQ-F1OzgnVB-vrREg0EyPy2WVyE2okg4cA=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/2Nuu6V2kWzsY5GjL7

*Description:*
Bryn Celli Ddu is a prehistoric site on the Welsh island of Anglesey located near Llanddaniel Fab. Its name means 'the mound in the dark grove'. It was archaeologically excavated between 1928 and 1929. Visitors can get inside the mound through a stone passage to the burial chamber, and it is the centrepiece of a major Neolithic Scheduled Monument in the care of Cadw. The presence of a mysterious pillar within the burial chamber, the reproduction of the 'Pattern Stone', carved with sinuous serpentine designs, and the fact that the site was once a henge with a stone circle, and may have been used to plot the date of the summer solstice have all attracted much interest.

_Listing 6 of 50_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Tredegar House
*Country:* wales
*Region:* south wales
*County:* Newport
*Type:* historic-house
*Period:* 17th century
*Coords:* 51.56157222, -3.02916667
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tredegar_House
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Tredegar%20House/@51.5597,-3.0167,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZN8U_w-VGHIKzyyPaBLpnKuUXRXcJK38fGzOZFqo9cO3NM70mjV62Rgi4DcwmmJuH8g7A6arndmnY2-6XcDzGTqeZ-4NSQo8tDSimZVRx41VM4nj7k0JE-k8GD-yUoXPfrvjGMaMEHmjcYqhg=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/K9mWKJF9JF2GXejeA

*Description:*
Tredegar House is a 17th-century Charles II-era country house in Coedkernew, on the southwestern edge of Newport, Wales. For over five hundred years it was home to the Morgan family, later Lords Tredegar, one of the most powerful and influential families in the area. Described as "the grandest and most exuberant country house in Monmouthshire" and one of the "outstanding houses of the Restoration period in the whole of Britain", the mansion stands in a reduced landscaped garden of 90 acres (0.36 km2). The property became a Grade I listed building on 3 March 1952 and has been under the care of the National Trust since March 2012. The park surrounding the house is designated Grade II* on the Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales.

_Listing 7 of 50_

Described as "the grandest and most exuberant country house in Monmouthshire" and one of the "outstanding houses of the Restoration period in the whole of Britain", the mansion stands in a reduced landscaped garden of 90 acres (0.36 km2). items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Hill of Tara
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* irelands ancient east
*County:* County Meath
*Type:* castle
*Period:* Neolithic-Medieval
*Coords:* 53.5775, -6.61194444
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hill_of_Tara
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Hill%20of%20Tara/@53.578,-6.612,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Stone_of_Destiny_2018-07-24.jpg/330px-Stone_of_Destiny_2018-07-24.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/cnNHNkiTm6FqKf8h9


*Description:*
The Hill of Tara is a hill and ancient ceremonial and burial site near Skryne in County Meath, Ireland. Tradition identifies the hill as the inauguration place and seat of the High Kings of Ireland; it also appears in Irish mythology. Tara consists of numerous monuments and earthworks—dating from the Neolithic to the Iron Age—including a passage tomb, burial mounds, round enclosures, a standing stone, and a ceremonial avenue. There is also a church and graveyard on the hill. Tara forms part of a larger ancient landscape and Tara itself is a protected national monument under the care of the Office of Public Works, an agency of the Irish Government.

_Listing 8 of 50_

There is also a church and graveyard on the hill. Tara forms part of a larger ancient landscape and Tara itself is a protected national monument under the care of the Office of Public Works, an agency of the Irish Government.

*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMKyXchhfOQiU9vYW9ADn01wUvD4yWfcStoNJtrVZHgk4i90gAjc3sQu9z-u5GDxfFYikJMmED-nppC5UE6__xg-8MYjqQcWimehOIqvWua_ezKO3FQ-o9eaDkUPqmp76iVlUaTkWYHigM9Zg=s4800-w1200

*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places - https://maps.app.goo.gl/RDSWhm4i4dFLpoLa6

*Description:*
Blarney Castle is a medieval stronghold in Blarney, a town in Cork, Ireland. Though earlier fortifications were built on the same spot, the current keep was built by the MacCarthy of Muskerry dynasty, a cadet branch of the Kings of Desmond, and dates from 1446. The Blarney Stone is among the machicolations of the castle.

_Listing 9 of 50_
